title: Chapter Two: The First Journal
content: The stairs to the attic had not been climbed in my lifetime.

I knew this because each step announced it — a deep, querulous creak that rose through the soles of my feet and into my chest, as though the house itself were clearing its throat after two centuries of silence. I climbed with one hand on the wall, fingertips tracing wallpaper that peeled in long pale ribbons, and with each step the air grew thinner, older, more reluctant to be breathed.

The attic door was not locked. It was worse than locked — it was forgotten. The brass knob was veiled in a skin of dust so thick it felt like velvet when I turned it. The hinges screamed, and I flinched, and then I was inside.

Amber light fell through a single cracked window and lay across the floor like a laid-down garment. Outside, the valley was choked with fog — white and featureless, a sea that had swallowed every landmark and left only this small house standing like a lighthouse nobody tended. Dust motes hung suspended in the light, frozen, seemingly unbothered by my intrusion. The air smelled of all the years that had been sealed here: dry rot, mouse droppings, the mineral tang of old nails. And beneath that, faint as a held breath — lavender.
I followed the scent.

Near the window, one of the floorboards sat slightly proud of its neighbors — a gap no wider than a thumbnail between it and the next board, but enough to catch the light differently. Enough to whisper *here*. I knelt. The floor was cold through my jeans, that deep stone cold that seeps up from foundations older than the country. I pressed my fingers into the gap and pried.
The board came up easily. Too easily. As if it had been waiting.

Beneath it: a leather journal, small enough to nestle in my palm like a prayer book. The leather was cracked and stiff, flaking when my thumb traced the spine. I lifted it out and the smell hit me — old paper, yes, the vanilla-acid tang of decomposition, but layered over with something alive. Lavender. Fresh lavender, impossible after two hundred and sixty-four years. And beneath the lavender, something saline and human. Tears, I thought. It smelled like tears.
I opened it.

The first pages were cipher — columns of symbols I didn't recognize, arranged with the meticulous care of someone who had been forced to invent her own alphabet because no existing one could be trusted. I turned past them, and the cipher gave way to English as though the writer could no longer contain the plain truth of it. The handwriting shifted from controlled to urgent, letters leaning forward like a woman running.
*5 March, 1762. He tells me my voice is too sharp. That women should be soft. That my silence is a kindness I give him. I have tried to be kind. God help me, I have tried.*
*12 March, 1762. I sewed my own name into the hem of my dress so I would not forget it. He did not notice. He does not look at what I make.*

*21 March, 1762. I can feel the truth pressing against my teeth. If I open my mouth it will pour out like water and he will drown in it and it will be my fault. I place my hand on the garden wall instead. The stones accept what I cannot say. They glow, briefly, with the things I have given them. He does not see this either.*
I read the last entry and the world dissolved.

There was no transition — no dimming, no warning shimmer. One moment I was cross-legged on the attic floor with a book in my lap, and the next I was *falling*, not through space but through time, through the membrane that separates now from then, and the amber light of the attic became green-gold garden light and the smell of dust became soil and bruised leaves and
*I was there.*

1762. I knew it the way you know your own name — intimately, without question. The garden was behind a house I had never seen but recognized instantly: lower windows shuttered, upper windows watching like dark eyes. A stone wall bordered the garden, and the stones were warm, and I could feel them humming with imprisoned truths even from where I floated — outside, above, unable to touch.
Maren knelt in the turned earth.

She was smaller than I had imagined. Dark hair pulled back so tightly it must have hurt. Hands pressed flat against the soil as though she were trying to push herself through to the other side of the world. And from her lips — from her mouth, her beautiful mouth, the mouth that could seal truth into words and make promises unbreakable and echo back every lie ever spoken — ivy grew.

Living ivy. Green tendrils that curled from between her lips and wound around her jaw and crept along her cheekbones and pinned her silence in place. The leaves were small and tender and bright, and they pulsed faintly with each breath she could not release as sound. I could smell them — green and sharp, the smell of living things colonizing dead places.

Her husband stood over her. He was tall and plain and unremarkable in every way except the casual authority with which occupied space. He looked down at Maren the way one looks at a dog that has finally stopped barking.
"Quiet now," he said. "That's better."
And he walked away.

I tried to scream. I tried to throw myself between them, to claw the ivy from her mouth, to *tell her* — but I had no voice here, no body, no weight. I was nothing but watching. And Maren — Maren pressed her hand to the garden wall.

The stones *blazed*. Not with light, but with something deeper — a resonance I could feel in my sternum, in my teeth, in the marrow of whatever ghost-form I inhabited. Truth pulsed into the stone. I could almost read them: *I am here. I am still here. I have a voice. I have a voice. I have a voice.*
Each pulse was weaker than the last.

She pressed her hand to the wall and the truths left her and the ivy grew tighter and her shoulders curved inward and I watched the breaking happen, not all at once but in pieces — the way a shoreline loses itself to the sea, grain by grain, until one day there is no shoreline at all. Piece by piece, Maren Buena's spirit left her eyes. Her hand fell from the wall. The ivy bloomed tiny white flowers at the corners of her mouth, and she did not even flinch.
She had learned to be kind.
I came back screaming.

Or — I thought I screamed. What came out was a sound halfway between a gasp and a sob, and then I was on the attic floor with the journal in my lap and tears running down my face and I could taste them — salt and grief and the metallic edge of rage. My fingers were cramped around the leather cover so tightly the cracked surface bit into my palms. The floor was still cold beneath me. The light was still amber. The dust had not moved.
But the journal was warm.

The warmth spread from the pressed flowers — I could feel them now, the thin papery weight of petals between the pages, still faintly fragrant, still somehow alive — and into my hands, up my wrists, pooling in my chest like swallowed sunlight. The glow was subtle; I would have missed it if I hadn't been looking. But the edges of the pages were luminous, just barely, the way skin is luminous when you hold a flashlight behind your fingers.
And I *felt* it unlock.

There is no other word. Something that had been sealed in me — something that had been waiting, perhaps, for exactly this moment — opened like a door. Voice-binding. I knew the name for it the way I knew my own pulse. The ability to seal truth into words. To make promises unbreakable. To echo back what was said, so that a lie confronted its speaker in their own voice. It was Maren's gift. It was now mine, passed forward through leather and lavender and the uncontainable ache of a woman who could not speak.
I wiped my face with the back of my hand and looked down at the journal again.
A new page.
Not written. Not printed. *Manifested* — the ink still gleaming wet, the letters forming themselves as I watched, rising from the paper like something surfacing from deep water.
*FOR THE SEVENTH. USE MY VOICE.*

I closed the journal. I pressed it against my chest. I sat on the floor of that attic and I breathed, and the breathing tasted like dust and lavender and old paper, and I did not feel armed. I did not feel chosen. I felt like a woman who had just watched another woman's soul fracture in real time, and the grief of it was so heavy I couldn't carry it sitting down.
So I stood. I stumbled. I took the journal with me.

Down the stairs — each step groaning beneath me, indignant at the double crossing. Through the second floor, past Nadine's door. I lifted my hand to knock and then let it fall. A thin line of light showed beneath her doorframe; she was awake, probably. She was always awake, these nights. But the words I would need to explain were too large for my throat, and I could hear her silence through the door — that particular quality of not-responding that meant *I am here, but I cannot help you with this.*
I went outside instead.

The night air hit me like a hand across the face — cold and wet, carrying the mineral smell of fog and the green decay of the valley below. I stood on the front step and breathed it in and felt the journal still warm against my sternum where I'd tucked it inside my jacket. The fog was thick, a white wall pressing against the cottage on all sides, and I let it fill my lungs and cool the fire that Maren's memory had lit beneath my ribs.
And then the fog parted.

Not gradually. Not the way fog usually moves — drifting, reforming, lazy. This was deliberate. A corridor opened through the white, long and straight, and at the end of it — at the end of the lane where the road bent toward the village — a figure.

Tall. Dark-haired. Walking toward my cottage with the easy confidence of someone who had always lived here, always walked this road, always known exactly where he was going. The fog closed behind him as though it were a door.
He stopped under the streetlamp.

The light caught him and I saw — God, I saw everything. The sharp geometry of his face. The way his dark hair fell across his forehead like it had been placed there by someone who cared about such things. The line of his shoulders, relaxed but certain. The stillness of him, the way he stood as though the world arranged itself around his comfort.
He smiled.

It was like the whole world tilted. Like gravity had a new center, and it was standing under a streetlamp fifty feet away, and every cell in my body was calculating the distance. I felt the pull in my sternum, in my spine, in the roots of my teeth. Not desire — not exactly. Something more structural than desire. Something that had been built into me before I was born, written into the Buena bloodline in ink older than Maren's cipher.
Julian Cross had arrived.
He extended a hand. Not toward me specifically — just outward, an open palm, the universal gesture of greeting. Of harmlessness. Of *I come in peace.*
"I just arrived in town," he said. "I couldn't sleep. The fog, you know?"

His voice was warm honey poured over cold iron. I heard the sweetness first — the rich amber tone that made the words feel like an embrace — and then the iron beneath it, the structural hardness that the sweetness was designed to conceal. Or not conceal, exactly. Contain. The way a scabbard contains a blade.

He was early. The curse said twenty-two. I was barely twenty-two — three days past my birthday, the number still fresh on my tongue like a label I hadn't learned to wear. And here he was, standing in the fog, offering me his hand with the easy certainty of a man who had never once considered the possibility of rejection.
I did not take his hand.
But I wanted to.

I wanted to with a specificity that frightened me — not the vague pull of attraction but a gravitational certainty, as though my body recognized something in his that it had been designed to fit against. I could feel the voice-binding pulsing in my chest, Maren's gift, the ability to seal truth into words, and I thought: *if I speak now, I might say something I can never take back.*
The fog pressed in around us. The journal was warm against my heart.
He was still smiling.

Knowledge is not armor. I knew what he was. I knew what the curse required. I knew what had happened to Maren and to every Aurelia before me who had stood in this exact position and felt this exact pull and made the choice that I would now have to make. I knew all of it — and still my hand twitched at my side, still my body leaned fractionally forward, still some ancient ineradicable part of me whispered *yes*.
I did not take his hand.
Not yet.
